title: Question: Will verilator properly handle true dual port RAMs?
body:
---


Author Name: **Jonathon Donaldson**
Original Redmine Message: 1471 from https://www.veripool.org



---

I have a systemverilog module which infers a true dual port RAM successfully on both Xilinx and Altera FPGAs. However, when I verilate the code I get some nasty-sounding warnings.
```
%Warning-MULTIDRIVEN: ../mem_lib/generic_ram.sv:78: Signal has multiple driving blocks: v.my_mem.dpram
%Warning-MULTIDRIVEN: ../mem_lib/generic_ram.sv:141: ... Location of first driving block
%Warning-MULTIDRIVEN: ../mem_lib/generic_ram.sv:109: ... Location of other driving block
%Warning-MULTIDRIVEN: Use "/* verilator lint_off MULTIDRIVEN */" and lint_on around source to disable this message.

```
If I check the verilator manual I see the following:
```
MULTIDRIVEN
     Warns that the specified signal comes from multiple always blocks. This is often unsupported by synthesis tools, and is considered bad style. It will also cause longer runtimes due to reduced optimizations.

```
That's pretty strong language. :) The code I'm using to infer the true dual port RAM was taking directly from both Xilinx/Altera reference manuals. I'm curious...how does verilator expect me to describe a true dual port RAM without having multiple drivers? I can't think of any other way you could code it that would make sense...

Note that I'm talking about a _true_ dual port RAM, not just a dual port RAM. A true dual port RAM has two input clocks. One clock for port A and another clock for port B. Hence there are two always blocks driving the 'dpram' memory. Hence there are multiple drivers.

I can get rid of the warning using "-Wno-MULTIDRIVEN" but my concern is if I should expect verilator to simulate this RAM properly given the above warnings and strong language in the verilator manual.

Also, how does verilator handle write/write and read/write collisions where two actions occur on the same clock cycle of the two different input clocks on both ports?

Thanks!!
code_blocks:
%Warning-MULTIDRIVEN: ../mem_lib/generic_ram.sv:78: Signal has multiple driving blocks: v.my_mem.dpram
%Warning-MULTIDRIVEN: ../mem_lib/generic_ram.sv:141: ... Location of first driving block
%Warning-MULTIDRIVEN: ../mem_lib/generic_ram.sv:109: ... Location of other driving block
%Warning-MULTIDRIVEN: Use "/* verilator lint_off MULTIDRIVEN */" and lint_on around source to disable this message.

MULTIDRIVEN
     Warns that the specified signal comes from multiple always blocks. This is often unsupported by synthesis tools, and is considered bad style. It will also cause longer runtimes due to reduced optimizations.
